intro:
### TL;DR (read this if nothing else)
- Three papers dropped this week all attacking the same problem: LLM judges are biased, and everyone's pretending they aren't. Solutions range from taxonomy + training (JudgeBiasBench) to formal math guarantees (A-BB) to clever scheduling (CyclicJudge).
- The classic MT-Bench/Chatbot Arena paper is still the foundation everything above builds on. If you haven't read it recently, you should.
footer:
## 🎯 Eval Landscape

This week tells a clear story: LLM-as-judge is the dominant paradigm, and the field is now in "fix the problems we've been ignoring" mode. Three independent teams attacked judge bias from taxonomy (JudgeBiasBench), formal theory (A-BB), and scheduling (CyclicJudge). The fact that all three landed in the same week suggests the community hit a tipping point where judge reliability became too embarrassing to keep ignoring. The next frontier is combining these approaches: formal bounds + bias-aware training + multi-judge rotation. Whoever builds that stack wins.

papers.title: Your LLM Judge Has 12 Different Ways to Be Wrong
papers.vibe_label: Finally someone mapped the full bias landscape instead of playing whack-a-mole
papers.water_cooler: Turns out your LLM judge has more biases than a Fox News anchor. Someone finally counted them all.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** A full taxonomy of LLM judge biases plus training methods to actually fix them.

**The problem:** Everyone knows LLM judges are biased (position bias, verbosity bias, etc.), but studies keep looking at 1-2 biases in isolation under one judge format. Nobody had a complete picture across both generative and discriminative judges.

**The trick:** JudgeBiasBench defines 12 bias types across 4 dimensions and uses a controlled bias injection pipeline to create evaluation instances. Then they train judges to separate "is this actually good?" from "does this trigger my biases?" using RL for generative judges and contrastive learning for discriminative ones.

**Numbers:** Current judges show significant and diverse bias patterns across the board. The debiasing training reduces biases while keeping general evaluation quality mostly intact.

**The catch:** Training-based debiasing means you need to retrain every time you discover a new bias type.

papers.title: Math Says Your Judge Is Biased, But Here's a Guarantee Anyway
papers.vibe_label: The first paper to bring real formal guarantees to LLM-as-judge. Feels like the field growing up
papers.water_cooler: Did you see someone finally put math behind LLM judge bias? Like, actual provable bounds, not just vibes.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** Formal mathematical framework that bounds how much bias can affect your LLM judge rankings, even for unknown bias types.

**The problem:** As we move toward autonomous AI feedback loops, we need LLM judges with actual guarantees, not just "GPT-4 agrees with humans 80% of the time, probably." Especially when bias vectors might be adversarially discovered.

**The trick:** Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB) is an algorithmic framework that wraps around any LLM judge and formally guarantees that bias impact stays below a threshold. Think of it like a statistical safety net: you don't need to eliminate every bias, you just need to prove the damage is bounded. Evaluated on Arena-Hard-Auto with 4 judges.

**Numbers:** Achieves (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias bounds while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings. Most judge-bias combos exceed 80% correlation. Code released.

**The catch:** 61% correlation on the low end is rough. You're trading ranking accuracy for formal guarantees, and that tradeoff isn't always worth it.

papers.title: Round-Robin Your Judges Like a Fantasy Football Draft
papers.vibe_label: Elegant and practical. This should be the default for anyone running LLM-as-judge evals
papers.water_cooler: CyclicJudge literally eliminates judge bias for free. Why isn't everyone doing this?
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** Use multiple judges in a round-robin schedule to cancel out bias at the same cost as using one judge.

**The problem:** Single-judge evaluations have systematic biases that don't go away with more test scenarios. These biases are often the same magnitude as the actual model differences you're trying to measure. Your leaderboard might be ranking judge preferences, not model quality.

**The trick:** Variance decomposition splits benchmark scores into scenario, generation, judge, and residual components. CyclicJudge assigns judges to scenarios in round-robin fashion, which mathematically eliminates judge bias. Each judge only evaluates once per cycle, so total cost equals single-judge evaluation. It's the eval equivalent of rotating referees.

**Numbers:** Validated on MT-Bench and MindEval across general and domain-specific settings. Bias elimination works as predicted by the theory.

**The catch:** You need multiple judge models available. If all your judges share the same blind spots (they're all fine-tuned from the same base), rotation helps less than you'd hope.

papers.title: The Paper That Started the Judge Wars
papers.vibe_label: One of the 5 most influential eval papers ever written. If you work in LLM evaluation and haven't read this, what are you doing?
papers.water_cooler: All three bias papers this week are basically sequels to the MT-Bench paper. It's the MCU of LLM evaluation.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** The foundational paper that proved strong LLMs can replace human evaluators for open-ended chat assessment.

**The problem:** In 2023, we had no good way to evaluate chat-based LLMs. Static benchmarks (MMLU, etc.) didn't capture what makes a chatbot actually useful. Human evaluation was expensive and slow. The field needed something scalable.

**The trick:** Two benchmarks in one paper. MT-Bench: 80 multi-turn questions across 8 categories, graded by GPT-4. Chatbot Arena: crowdsourced blind pairwise battles where humans vote. They showed GPT-4 judge achieves >80% agreement with humans, which is the same as human-human agreement. They also cataloged the biases (position, verbosity, self-enhancement) that all three new papers above are now trying to fix.

**Numbers:** 80%+ agreement between GPT-4 and human preferences. MT-Bench scores correlated strongly with Arena Elo. The Arena has since collected millions of votes and become the de facto LLM ranking.

**The catch:** The 80% agreement number gets cited like gospel, but it's measured on a specific distribution. Different domains, languages, and tasks can see much lower agreement. Also, GPT-4 is the judge AND a competitor, which is... awkward.

intro:
### TL;DR (read this if nothing else)
- Autorubric drops a unified Python framework for rubric-based LLM evaluation with psychometric reliability metrics, few-shot calibration, and bias mitigations. The first serious attempt to standardize how we grade with LLMs.
- One-Eval turns "evaluate my model on reasoning tasks" into a fully automated pipeline: natural language in, traceable benchmark results out. Eval-as-a-service is arriving.
- MASEval proves framework choice matters as much as model choice for agentic systems. Your LangGraph vs AutoGen decision isn't just engineering taste, it's a performance variable.
footer:
## 🎯 Eval Landscape

Two threads this week. First: the tooling layer is maturing fast. Autorubric, One-Eval, and MASEval are all trying to make evaluation less of a one-off artisanal craft and more of an engineering discipline with reusable infrastructure. Second: the contamination/saturation problem keeps driving innovation (Raw Corpora to Benchmarks, MMLU's death). The community is converging on a truth that Jun probably already knows: static benchmarks have a shelf life measured in months, not years. The winning strategy is pipelines that generate fresh benchmarks on demand, not static test sets you publish once and pray nobody trains on.

papers.title: Finally, a Grown-Up Framework for Rubric-Based Eval
papers.vibe_label: Not flashy, but this is the kind of infrastructure work that quietly makes the whole field better. Think of it as pytest for LLM evaluation
papers.water_cooler: Someone finally made the Django of LLM evaluation instead of everyone hand-rolling their own eval scripts.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** Open-source Python framework that standardizes every rubric-based LLM evaluation technique scattered across dozens of papers.

**The problem:** Everyone's doing rubric-based eval differently. Some use binary criteria, some ordinal, some mix both. Multi-judge ensembles use different aggregation. Nobody agrees on how to measure reliability. The techniques exist but the implementation is copy-paste chaos.

**The trick:** Autorubric unifies everything under one roof: binary/ordinal/nominal criteria with configurable weights, multi-judge ensembles (majority, weighted, unanimous, any-vote), few-shot calibration with verdict-balanced sampling, and bias mitigations (position shuffling, length penalties, per-criterion atomic evaluation). It also brings psychometric reliability metrics (Cohen's kappa, weighted kappa) from social science into LLM eval. Plus production niceties: caching, checkpointing, rate limiting, cost tracking.

**Numbers:** Validated on three benchmarks (RiceChem, ResearcherBench, CHARM-100) producing results consistent with published scores. They also release CHARM-100, a 100-sample chatbot eval dataset with ground truth across all criterion types.

**The catch:** Framework adoption is the hard part. The techniques aren't novel individually; the value is in the unified implementation. If your team already has a working eval pipeline, the switching cost might not be worth it.

papers.title: "Evaluate My Model on Reasoning" and the System Just Does It
papers.vibe_label: The vision is right, the execution is early. If this matures, it could become the standard way teams run evals. Right now it's more proof-of-concept than production-ready
papers.water_cooler: There's now an AI agent whose entire job is evaluating other AI agents. We're getting recursive.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** An agentic system that converts natural-language evaluation requests into executable, traceable benchmark workflows.

**The problem:** Running proper LLM evals is a slog. You pick benchmarks, clone repos, wrangle dataset schemas, configure metrics, and then pray everything is reproducible. Most practitioners either skip proper eval or do it badly because the friction is too high.

**The trick:** Three-stage pipeline. NL2Bench parses your plain-English request ("evaluate GPT-4o on medical reasoning") into structured benchmark plans. BenchResolve handles dataset acquisition, schema normalization, and execution. Metrics & Reporting does task-aware metric selection and generates decision-oriented reports, not just numbers. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints let you review and roll back. Every sample keeps an evidence trail for debugging.

**Numbers:** Successfully executes end-to-end evaluations from diverse natural-language requests with minimal user effort. The real win is reproducibility: same request, same results, full audit trail.

**The catch:** "Natural language to benchmark" is a leaky abstraction. For non-standard evals or custom datasets, you'll probably still need to get your hands dirty. Also, benchmark selection is only as good as the system's benchmark catalog.

papers.title: Your Agent Framework Is a Confounding Variable
papers.vibe_label: This needed to exist yesterday. Anyone building agent systems should be evaluating the system, not just the model. The finding alone is worth the read
papers.water_cooler: MASEval found that switching from AutoGen to LangGraph can matter as much as switching from GPT-4 to Claude. Let that sink in.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** Framework-agnostic evaluation library that treats the entire agentic system (not just the model) as the unit of analysis. Finds that framework choice matters as much as model choice.

**The problem:** Current agent benchmarks are model-centric: they fix the agentic setup and only swap the LLM. But in practice, your choice of framework (LangGraph vs AutoGen vs smolagents), orchestration logic, topology, and error handling all affect performance. Nobody was measuring that.

**The trick:** MASEval wraps any framework's agents in a standard interface, exposes message histories for tracing, simulates users for multi-turn benchmarks, and computes metrics via a filter-then-score pattern. They ran systematic comparisons: 3 benchmarks x 3 models x 3 frameworks. The library is MIT-licensed.

**Numbers:** Framework choice matters as much as model choice. That's the headline. Your decision between LangGraph and AutoGen isn't just a developer experience preference; it's a performance variable you should be benchmarking.

**The catch:** 3x3x3 is a small grid. We need bigger studies to know if this holds across more diverse tasks and frameworks. Also, framework versions change fast, so results have a short shelf life.

papers.title: Contamination-Proof Benchmarks from Raw Text
papers.vibe_label: Solid infrastructure paper. Contamination is the eval field's biggest ongoing headache, and deterministic pipelines that avoid it completely are the right direction
papers.water_cooler: Someone built a benchmark generator that doesn't use any LLM. The contamination problem just became optional.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** A deterministic pipeline that transforms raw domain text into completion-style benchmarks without using any LLM or human annotator, sidestepping contamination entirely.

**The problem:** Domain-specific benchmarks (law, medicine, education) keep getting contaminated. They're also mostly multiple-choice, which has known biases. And you can't fairly compare base models to chat models on MCQ-format benchmarks because chat models are trained to pick letters.

**The trick:** Extract domain-specific keywords from a raw corpus, build prompt-target pairs where domain terms are the prediction targets, then measure if the LLM can complete the prompt correctly. No LLM in the loop means no contamination from the benchmark generator. The pipeline can auto-update with new domain data. And because it's completion-style (not MCQ), base and chat models compete on equal footing.

**Numbers:** Model performance on their auto-generated benchmarks significantly correlates with expert-curated benchmarks. They also show interesting patterns in how knowledge is acquired during domain-adaptive, continual, and general pretraining.

**The catch:** Completion-style benchmarks test knowledge recall, not reasoning. A model could "know" all the domain terms but still fail at applying them. Also, keyword extraction quality depends heavily on the input corpus.

papers.title: The Benchmark That Conquered the World (and Then Died)
papers.vibe_label: for historical impact. 🔥 for current utility. Every eval researcher should understand MMLU's lifecycle because it's the cautionary tale for every benchmark being designed today
papers.water_cooler: MMLU went from 'the gold standard' to 'basically useless' in 3 years. That's the half-life of every benchmark we're building right now.
papers.markdown:
**One-liner:** 57-subject multiple-choice exam covering everything from abstract algebra to virology. The first benchmark to seriously test breadth of knowledge. Now every frontier model scores 90%+ and it tells us nothing.

**The problem (in 2020):** GPT-3 had just dropped and people realized language models might actually "know things." But how do you test knowledge breadth across dozens of domains? Existing benchmarks were narrow. MMLU proposed testing models the way we test humans: standardized exams across 57 subjects, 4 levels of difficulty.

**The trick:** 15,908 questions harvested from real exams (practice tests, AP courses, graduate-level). Four-choice MCQ format. Zero-shot and few-shot evaluation. The simplicity was the point: no fancy prompting, no chain-of-thought, just "pick A/B/C/D." Subjects range from elementary math to professional medicine to moral philosophy.

**Numbers:** GPT-3 scored ~43% (random is 25%). In 2024, GPT-4 hit 86.4%. By early 2026, every frontier model exceeds 90%. The benchmark has been fully saturated. The LocalLLaMA community now explicitly lists it as "dead, no signal left."

**The catch:** MMLU's problems go beyond saturation. Contamination is rampant (training data overlap is hard to verify across 57 subjects). The MCQ format favors models trained on instruction-following. Some questions have disputed answers. And "knowledge breadth" turned out to be less interesting than "can it actually reason?" But MMLU's real legacy is methodological: it showed the field that comprehensive benchmarks need to be living, updatable things, not static snapshots. MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, and the whole "harder benchmarks" wave exist because MMLU died so they could live.
